observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:
Inventory Committee to continue and further intensify their efforts to complete the conduct of physical inventory of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) and Investment Property, including a reconciliation of the physical count with the property and accounting records, as required by Chapter 4 of the Handbook on Property and Supply Management System and Paragraph 4 of Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. Any discrepancies should be thoroughly investigated, cleared, immediately reconciled, and supported with written explanations from accountable persons, if necessary.
The procedures on one-time cleansing of PPE account balances are recommended per COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal General Services Office to enhance further their documentation and record-keeping practices related to PPE and Investment Property by preparing and continuously maintaining and updating PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively. Both offices should ensure that all movements of PPE are properly recorded and documented to facilitate accurate reconciliation and reporting; and
Relevant members of the Inventory Committee and Disposal Committee to undergo training on proper inventory procedures, reconciliation processes and record-keeping practices, as well as disposal procedures, including appraisal, documentation, and methods of disposal, if necessary.

Management Comment/Action:
The Management concurred with the audit recommendations and committed to immediately conducting a thorough review of the dormant balances under the Construction in Progress (CIP) account. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant shall ensure the maintenance of individual Subsidiary Ledgers for each project implemented by the LGU.

Management Comment/Action:
Management committed to implement the audit recommendations and will ensure the reclassification of the completed project to the appropriate PPE account, along with the recording of required depreciation expenses. A reporting mechanism between the Municipal Accountant and Engineering Office will also be established to ensure timely recognition of construction projects.

Management Comment/Action:
Management agreed with the recommendations. The Municipal Accountant will record the ongoing projects under the appropriate CIP account and coordinate with the Engineering Office to ensure timely reporting and recording of project statuses, even without actual billings.

Management Comment/Action:
Management confirmed strict compliance with COA Circular 2024-006, item 3.3, wherein tangible items costing below ₱50,000 are recorded as inventory upon purchase and expensed upon issuance to end-users. Management also assured that Stock Ledger Cards are maintained for procured items not yet issued.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to verify and validate the existence of receivables, determine the debtors, and assess their collectibility. If deemed uncollectible, request authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts following COA Circular No. 2016-005. Further, if the receivables lack supporting documents, require the execution of a sworn certification stating the reasons for the loss of documents, and the person/s responsible thereof; and
Ensure that subsidiary ledgers are maintained for receivables in accordance with Section 4(e) of the NGAS to provide detailed information on accounts and facilitate collection efforts in the future.

Management Comment/Action:
Management explained that the absence of a certified list of taxpayers was due to the non-maintenance of such record in prior years. In line with the ongoing general revision being undertaken by the Municipal Assessor’s Office, efforts are currently focused on building a comprehensive taxpayer database. The Office targets to finalize the certified list by the end of Calendar Year 2025, in compliance with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that Management:
Maintain continuous compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013 in the proper grant, utilization and liquidation of the fund transfers from NGAs;
Ensure strict adherence to the guidelines specified in the respective Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) of each project while considering the principles outlined in P.D. 1445 regarding the management and utilization of government resources; and
Monitor project implementation closely to ensure adherence to the established timeline and that projects remain on track.

We recommended that Management coordinate with the Source Agencies to determine the correct disposition of the fund transfers and secure a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or any formal documentation to establish the fund's purpose, intended use, and conditions for its allocation.

Management Comment/Action:
Management reported that beginning in 2023, subsidiary ledgers for Due to GSIS accounts were separated and monitored, with monitoring sheets provided to the Auditor. Negative beginning balances may be due to recording errors and not necessarily over-remittances. Management committed to reconcile the balances, identify the root causes, and make necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Accounting Office:
Analyze and reconcile the individual SL accounts under the Due to Pag-IBIG account to identify and resolve the root cause of the discrepancies, including any discrepancies in withholding or remittances made in FY 2024 and the previous years; and
Cause the necessary adjustments in the LGUs books in compliance with IPSAS and COA regulations to reflect the accurate balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct a comprehensive reconciliation of the Due to BIR account with the corresponding BIR returns, official tax remittance forms, and transaction listings for December 2024, and for prior months, if necessary, to determine the accuracy of recorded balances and identify any overstatements or unremitted tax obligations; and
Based on the reconciliation results, take appropriate action by preparing the necessary adjusting entries to correct any overstatements, or by ensuring prompt remittance of any identified tax liabilities still due to the Bureau of Internal Revenue.

We recommended that Management direct the Accounting Office to:
Prepare and maintain complete and regularly updated subsidiary ledger for the Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable account, ensuring traceability and validity of recorded transactions; and
Conduct a review of the dormant accounts to validate the existence of claims and facilitate their proper disposition or settlement.

We recommended that Management:
Examine the payables which remained outstanding for more than two       years to confirm and establish valid claims and submit to the Audit Team pertinent documents to support their validity; otherwise, effect reversion of those accounts that have no valid claims;
Conduct a thorough review of all liability accounts to ensure they meet the criteria for recognition as per the provisions of IPSAS No. 1, namely: a) existence of a present obligation from past events, b) probability of outflow of resources embodying economic benefits, and c) reliable measurement of the obligation's cost/value; and
Ensure that all obligations are supported by valid claims, bills, relevant records, and valid proofs to warrant payment. This includes maintaining subsidiary ledgers for each creditor with adequate information to justify the existence of such obligations.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160;
Require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund; and
Ensure that the cash accruing from the Due from Other Funds is collected in full.

We recommended that Management require the:
Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) to ensure the timely implementation of PPAs under both current and continuing appropriations, and prioritize the execution of projects under the Special Trust Fund;
MDRRMO to regularly update the LDRRMF Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) and closely monitor physical and financial accomplishments to ensure timely project delivery; and
Municipal Treasury Office, Budget Office and Accounting Office to (1) Reclassify the unexpended MOOE from Continuing Appropriations to the Special Trust Fund in accordance with JMC No. 2013-1; (2) Revert to the General Fund all unutilized Special Trust Fund balances that have exceeded the five-year validity, as mandated by Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121; and (3) Reconcile and correct any misstatements in the LDRRMF balances to ensure proper fund accounting.

We recommended that Management conduct a thorough review of the current compensation rates for its JO personnel and comply with the prescribed minimum daily wage rates set forth under Wage Order No. RTWPB 2-23 by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards – II.

We recommended that the members of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) fully implement monitoring tools such as the HGDG and PIMME during GAD planning and budgeting, as well as in the preparation of the accomplishment report. This will ensure the actual expenditures are properly attributed to the GAD budget and that funded programs and projects are genuinely gender-responsive.

We recommended that Management:
Develop and enforce an internal memorandum or directive requiring the concerned office or personnel to submit purchase orders and perfected contracts on time; and
Implement a tracking system for the submission of purchase orders and perfected contracts.

We recommended that the Municipality of Cordon, in coordination with the Local School Board, being the one responsible in the formulation, approval, and utilization of the SEF Budget as provided under RA No. 7160 or the Local Government Code:
Strengthen planning and programming mechanisms to ensure that all appropriated funds, including continuing appropriations from prior years, are timely and fully utilized within the fiscal year, thereby avoiding idle or unspent balances;
Review and assess the implementation status of programs and projects with zero utilization, such as the Repairs and Maintenance of Machinery and Equipment, Indigenous Peoples Education (IPED), and MADRASA support programs, to determine bottlenecks and formulate corrective measures for their implementation:
From time to time, revisit the revised guidelines on the use of SEF under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure a more strategic and efficient formulation and utilization of resources for priority programs and projects;
Continuously ensure that the allocation of the budget takes into account the priority areas specified under Section 100(c) of R.A. No. 7160; and
For the purpose of hiring teachers chargeable against the SEF, the LSB in each province, city or municipality should utilize the list found in the Registry of Qualified Applicants (RQA).
We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Office provide the different schools, thru their district Supervisors, information as to their available appropriations for their prompt implementation. In turn, the different schools should maintain a registry of appropriations, obligations and balances to have updated information that will facilitate their disbursements of funds.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the proposed adjusting journal entry.

Apply effective cash programming to facilitate the proper handling of cash such that it is available when needed for payment of obligations and at the same time, optimize its utilization; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer instruct: 

All designated Collectors / Accountable Officers / Deputized Barangay Collectors through the Municipal Treasurer to remit their collections at the end of each business day or the next banking day to avoid misappropriation of cash. For field collectors, in cases where travel time from the places of assignment to the Treasurer’s Office is more than one day, turnover of collections shall be made once a week or as soon as the collections reach ₱5,000.00, whichever comes first; and

The Municipal Treasurer or Liquidating Officer to issue demand letter to Collectors / Tellers / Deputized Barangay Collectors if collections are not remitted at the period required.

Conduct the disposal of unserviceable properties soonest to avoid further decline of its value.

We recommended that management strengthen the GAD Focal Point System thru capacity building and coordination with other offices of the municipality and other stakeholders in order that the GAD plan and budget will be responsive to the gender issues and concerns of the locality within the context of the LGU mandate.

We recommended that management: a) implement the priority projects funded by the national government agencies; b) evaluate and review the existing balances in order to determine the proper disposition of these balances; c) coordinate with source agencies for proper disposition thereof, otherwise, request for realignment of these funds to be utilize for beneficial or meaningful projects; and d) return the remaining or unutilized amount on programs/projects which already have been completed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all accountable officers/collectors with unremitted collections and those who used the collections to refund and deposit the cash used and to refrain from using the collections for payment of various expenses of the municipality whether it is for donation or for cash advance. Remit in full their collections as required by laws and regulations to avoid possible loss or misappropriation of the fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) continue to practice restraint and prudence in the disbursement of funds, giving priority to highly necessary, high impact projects, programs and activities (PPAs); and (b) identify and implement revenue enhancing strategies to further improve collection efficiency.

We also recommended for the settlement of remaining cash advances specifically those that have been outstanding for over one year and more. Continue its policy on strict implementation of the rules on cash advances by not granting additional cash advances for those with balances, issuance of demand letters or withholding of salaries of the officials and employees concerned.

We recommended that the management reconcile the result of the actual physical count of the PPE with the records in the books of accounts of the accounting office and effect necessary adjustments after reconciliation has been made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to locate the documents pertaining to the payable accounts of the agency and revert to the unappropriated surplus all payable accounts outstanding for over 5 years that had not been claimed or without valid supporting documents and were outstanding for over 5 years.

We have recommended that management utilize the excise fund received by identifying and prioritizing the implementation of projects as embodied in the implementing guidelines in order to enhance farmer’s income.

We reiterated our recommendation that management remit the share on the real property tax collections of the Province of Isabela to prevent possible misuse of funds.     Management is likewise recommended to adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of P.D. 1445 and Section 307 of R.A. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit immediately the unexpended balance of the PDAF to the national government through the Bureau of the Treasury, in conformity with the Supreme Court’s decision.
We recommended that management require/implement immediate settlement of audit disallowances so that the irregularly disbursed funds of the municipality shall be recovered.
We recommended that management continue the withholding of taxes from suppliers and individuals and remit the amount withheld on time as required by BIR rules and regulations.

We have recommended that management: (a) Implement projects under the 20% Development Fund so that expected benefits due to the beneficiaries/constituents will be achieved or delivered on time; (b) Direct the Departments/Offices concerned to pursue the completion of the terms of reference and other studies in order for these projects to be implemented at the soonest time possible; and (c) Improve the planning and scheduling of work activities and manpower in order to facilitate completion of program of work of identified projects.
